message:
perf(prepare-cache): drop db_generation from prepared-statement cache key

Prepared-statement bytecode and template state depend on SQL text,
schema identity (schema_cookie + schema_generation), and the function
registry generation. They do not depend on row-data state.

Until now the prepared-cache key (and the corresponding validity check
in `PreparedStatementTemplate::is_valid`) also included
`memdb_visible_commit_seq` — which is bumped on every successful
commit. That meant every prepared statement invalidated across every
transaction boundary, even when nothing schema-relevant had changed.
Workloads that prepare-then-loop-over-commit (the flagship case is
`mt_mvcc_bench` firing thousands of INSERTs in a tight commit loop)
always missed the cache and paid the full re-lex + re-plan cost on
every iteration.

On the 2026-04-23 MT capture, that re-lex+re-plan work showed up as
a ~2pp cost in the Lexer + `write_ident` hot path.

Fix: remove `db_generation` from:
  1. `PreparedStatementTemplate.db_generation` field (unused after
     removal from `is_valid`)
  2. `PreparedStatementTemplate::from_prepared_statement` (no longer
     snapshots the commit seq)
  3. `PreparedStatementTemplate::is_valid` signature + body
  4. `Connection::compute_cache_key` signature + hash input
  5. `Connection::make_cache_key` call site
  6. Cache lookup site in `Connection::lookup_prepared`

Schema DDL is still correctly tracked: any `ALTER TABLE`, `CREATE
INDEX`, etc. bumps `schema_cookie` or `schema_generation`, which
remain in the cache key and invalidate prepared bytecode exactly when
it's structurally stale. Pure row-data commits no longer cause
spurious invalidation.

Inlined comments explain the rationale at both the `is_valid` and
`compute_cache_key` sites so a future refactor doesn't reintroduce
the generation into the key "for consistency".

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
